disease.name: Pediatric Pneumonia
disease.note: Adapted per WHO IMCI guidelines. For neonates <2 months, use neonatal sepsis/pneumonia protocol.

imciClassification.description: WHO IMCI: classify cough/difficult breathing into no pneumonia, pneumonia, or severe pneumonia based on chest indrawing and danger signs.

immediateDangerSigns.description: Head nodding in synchrony with respiration indicates severe distress in infants

additionalCriteria.description: Chest indrawing is the KEY sign for severe classification per IMCI
Stridor in a calm child indicates severe upper airway obstruction

nonSevereCriteria.description: Pneumonia (no severe features): cough or difficulty breathing + chest indrawing OR fast breathing, NO danger signs

noPneumoniaCriteria.description: No pneumonia: cough or cold without chest indrawing and without fast breathing
noPneumoniaCriteria.management: Cough/cold — no antibiotics. Symptomatic care only.

severityAssessment.severityLabel: IMCI: danger sign → SEVERE PNEUMONIA; indrawing OR fast breathing without danger signs → PNEUMONIA; neither → NO PNEUMONIA (cough/cold)

historyFeatures.note: Age-calculated tachypnoea threshold applied per IMCI

historyFeatures.description: One of WHO IMCI general danger signs

negativePredictor.description: Pure wheeze without fever reduces pneumonia probability strongly.

historyFeatures.note: Unimmunized or incomplete immunization increases risk of vaccine-preventable pneumonia

examFeatures.note: IMCI key sign for pneumonia classification
Specific sign of severe respiratory distress in young infants

riskFactors.note: PCV, Hib, measles vaccines are protective
Smoke from cooking fuels increases risk

management.nonSevere: High-dose amoxicillin PO 40-45 mg/kg/dose 12-hourly for 5 days
management.severe: IV Benzylpenicillin 50,000 IU/kg every 6h + Gentamicin 7.5 mg/kg once daily
management.treatmentFailure48h: Switch to IV Ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg once daily if not improving

subtypes.name: Viral Pneumonia
subtypes.management: Supportive care; oxygen if hypoxic. No routine antibiotics unless bacterial superinfection suspected.

subtypes.name: Atypical Pneumonia (Mycoplasma/Chlamydia)
subtypes.management: Macrolide (e.g., Azithromycin 10 mg/kg once daily for 3 days) if indicated.

subtypes.name: Aspiration Pneumonia
subtypes.management: Antibiotics covering anaerobes (e.g., Amoxicillin-clavulanate 45 mg/kg/dose 12-hourly). Address underlying cause. Consider thickened feeds, NG tube.

subtypes.name: Staphylococcal Pneumonia
subtypes.management: IV Flucloxacillin + Gentamicin; consider drainage of empyema.

subtypes.name: Pneumocystis jirovecii Pneumonia (PCP)
subtypes.management: High-dose co-trimoxazole IV (trimethoprim 20 mg/kg/day in 4 divided doses); oxygen; prednisolone if severe hypoxia (PaO2 <70 mmHg).

subtypes.name: Neonatal Pneumonia (<2 months)
subtypes.management: Ampicillin 50 mg/kg IV every 8h + Gentamicin 5 mg/kg IV once daily for 7-10 days. Admit to NICU.

bedside.name: Pulse oximetry
bedside.relevance: severity, hypoxia assessment, oxygen eligibility criteria

bedside.name: Respiratory rate counting (full one minute)
bedside.ageThresholdsNote: Use IMCI age-specific cut-offs: ≥50 for 2-11 months, ≥40 for 12-59 months

laboratory.name: FBC + CRP
laboratory.indication: all suspected pneumonia, to support bacterial aetiology

laboratory.name: Blood culture
laboratory.indication: severe pneumonia, toxic child, immunocompromised, empyema

laboratory.name: HIV test (PCR or rapid)
laboratory.indication: all children with pneumonia in high-prevalence settings, or as per protocol when suspect PCP

laboratory.name: GeneXpert (sputum/gastric aspirate)
laboratory.indication: chronic cough >14 days, TB contact, poor response to antibiotics

imaging.name: Chest X-ray PA (and lateral if needed)
imaging.indication: severe pneumonia, hypoxia, treatment failure, suspected effusion/empyema/cavitation, recurrent pneumonia

typicalFindingsBySubtype.pcp: bilateral diffuse interstitial opacities, may be normal early

advanced.name: CT chest
advanced.indication: complications not resolving, suspected foreign body, bronchiectasis

management.protocolReference: WHO IMCI Guidelines + Kenya Paediatric Management Guidelines, child 2-59 months

pathways.criteria: Cough or cold without chest indrawing and without fast breathing
treatment: Soothe throat and relieve cough with safe remedy
Counsel on when to return: if breathing becomes difficult, fast breathing develops, unable to feed, or condition worsens

pathways.criteria: Cough or difficult breathing + chest indrawing OR fast breathing (age-specific threshold), NO danger signs
treatment: High-dose amoxicillin PO 40-45 mg/kg/dose 12-hourly for 5 days
If wheeze present AND fast breathing → treat as pneumonia if first episode; if recurrent wheeze, consider asthma
pathways.followUp: Return immediately if: develops danger signs, not able to drink, becomes sicker. Review at 48 hours.

pathways.criteria: Any danger sign (SpO2 <90%, central cyanosis, unable to drink/breastfeed, AVPU abnormal, grunting, head nodding, lethargy) OR chest indrawing + any danger sign
treatment: IV Benzylpenicillin 50,000 IU/kg every 6h + Gentamicin 7.5 mg/kg once daily
Monitor SpO2, respiratory rate, indrawing, feeding, consciousness
Ensure adequate feeding (breastfeeding support or NG/IV if needed)
pathways.reassessment: At 48 hours: if worsening or no improvement, switch to IV Ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg once daily. Suspect staphylococcal if cavitation present – add Flucloxacillin.

pathways.criteria: Progression to danger signs, no improvement in RR/indrawing/fever/feeding at 48h, worsening at day 5

definitions.progression_to_severe: Child initially classified as pneumonia develops danger signs → admit and treat as severe
definitions.48h_failure: Severe pneumonia child worsening; switch to Ceftriaxone; if staph suspected use Flucloxacillin + Gentamicin
definitions.day5_failure: ≥3 of: fever >38°C, RR >60, cyanosis/SpO2 <90% unchanged, chest indrawing persistent, worsening CXR
definitions.chronic_cough_>14d: Suspect TB. Perform Mantoux, CXR, TB contact history, and consider TB treatment per guidelines.

oxygenCriteria: SpO2 <90% on room air → start oxygen via nasal prongs at 1-2 L/min
Wean oxygen once stable on room air for 4-6 hours

specialConsiderations.wheeze_branch: If wheeze present → if first episode with fever → treat as pneumonia; if recurrent wheeze and afebrile → consider asthma protocol
specialConsiderations.HIV_exposed: See separate HIV/PCP protocol. Early HIV testing, treat for PCP if hypoxic and failing standard antibiotics
specialConsiderations.SAM_child: WHO IMCI: all children with severe acute malnutrition and cough/difficulty breathing should be admitted and treated with IV antibiotics
specialConsiderations.age_under_2_months: Do not use this protocol; use integrated management of neonatal/young infant illness (IMNCI) protocol

actions: Get chest X-ray if not done
Increase probability of: empyema, resistant organism, staphylococcal pneumonia, TB, PCP (if HIV risk)
Consider HIV testing if not done

disease.emergencyPriority: if any danger sign present, immediate admission and oxygen
diagnosticClues: IMCI: chest indrawing OR fast breathing in child with cough/difficult breathing
exclusionClues: pure wheeze with no fever and recurrent episodes (asthma)
sudden onset with choking history (foreign body)
